text: Research into machine learning (ML) has seen an unprecedented surge in popularity over the last several decades. Despite this growth, researchers without a formal background in statistics often find it challenging to navigate the nuances of model evaluation and comparative analysis. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the most common evaluation metrics across a wide range of supervised learning tasks, including binary, multi-class, and multi-label classification, as well as regression, image segmentation, object detection, and information retrieval. We provide practical guidance on selecting appropriate statistical tests, generating sufficient metric data for robust testing, and interpreting the resulting statistical significance. Finally, we demonstrate these concepts through real-world applications, comparing the performance of convolutional neural networks in classifying lung infections from X-ray imagery and detecting malignant tumors in positron emission tomography (PET) scans.
